text_eng:
My topic for this course touches on randomness, and coincidentally (though most probably not coincidence at all), I am also considering randomness as part of another project for another course. I am a great admirer of serendipity. Actually, depending on your world view, you could consider every event in life to be random, and then there are happenings that you consider destiny. Every person you cross in the supermarket is random and negligible, but as soon as it is that certain person and it is not at all their area, it seems like a very random but symbolic experience. Destiny is just the selection of random events you choose to give value to, right? I still remember the quote by Milan Kundera, even though I read that book many years ago: Chance and chance alone has a message for us. Everything that occurs out of necessity, everything expected, repeated day in and day out, is mute. Only chance can speak to us.
Maybe it is just a very romantic view on life to give certain events a deeper meaning, to attach yourself, your day, your memory, your attention to this unforeseeable moment. Sometimes you have to make room in your everyday life to allow these encounters to happen, to let go of control and embrace everything that comes your way with the intention of it being something important. Sometimes it occurs in a moment where you least expect it, like in the supermarket from the example above. Some people do not think about the chain of causal events that may have led to the random situation, while others try to manipulate their and other people’s life to converge into a desired event. Maybe seeing something as random enhances its value? Appreciating the minute probability of it happening as if it meant it could still be taken away from you, as if the low chances also meant little time and presence. Or does it reduce the value of a person or a moment, as stochasticity equals meaninglessness? 
Anyways, just random thoughts on this topic ...

Nostalgia is a haven for me; I love going there, turning to it. I came across the term years ago in Milan Kundera’s Ignorance, it is formed by combining the Greek words nóstos—meaning ‘return home’—and álgos—meaning ‘pain’. Later, it says, the Spanish call it añoranza, derives from the Latin ignorare, meaning ‘to not know’. So, it emerged as the pain of not knowing, that was when our connection was formed; my homeland is far away, and I don't know what is happening there. Whilst it originated as an affliction, today it has evolved into something with a much broader meaning. For some, what makes it beautiful is the sense of having a place to return to amidst the absence of any vision for the future; for others, it is the fear that it might sever the connection with the present and lead one into a great delusion. For me, however, it behaves like a friend who has been by my side, at the same time acts like a stalker who occupies a space in my mind, constantly cropping up in what I consume and create. The words spoken by Nate’s character in the TV series Six Feet Under – “You can’t take a picture of this, it’s already gone” – had etched into my memory the idea that finding nostalgia happens as quickly as a photograph is captured, which I found ruthless. Much later, I realized that the panic of losing nostalgia was even more ruthless. Just like the fear experienced by the protagonist in The Worst Person in the World, when, as death approaches, he realizes his connection to the past has shifted from nostalgia to the loss of memories. But it is hard to know how much of the place your mind returns to is reality and how much is fiction. Yet, today where many things around us can be consumed so quickly, we are drawn into a hunger to feel profound emotions. Although feeling such a suffocating emotion and this state of revisiting the past can sometimes be painful, in a way, the pain itself is pleasurable precisely because it is so intense – for it makes one feel truly alive.

Originally planned as a zine, circumstances led Take your voice and put it in the blender to be adapted as a site-specific installation: Ruiz processed some of the artistic statements that accompany the works in the exhibition by repeatedly feeding them through an LLM with additional instructions to make the language more abstract, institutional or pretentious. The style transfer capabilities of LLMs, applied in a loop, distort the human voice of artists and generate outputs that, while still containing particles of the original ideas, fall into the uncanny valley. This, in turn, emphasizes how algorithmically generated text and the conventions of artspeak (what David Levine and Alix Rule called "International Art English") remove the details and flatten the features that distinguish a person's voice. By placing the computer generated text next to the original artistic statement, Ruiz attempts to draw the audience’s attention to the identity and the character present in each of the exhibit's works.

Printed posters display the entire source code used to generate the texts, a QR code that links to the corresponding Git repo, and a huge prompt that makes evident the mechanical nature of these reproductions. The fact that this Python code was written by Claude Code shows how, ironically, the usefulness of LLMs for writing computer programs exceeds by far its usefulness in more human, personal tasks.

This project turned the theoretical question of computer science into a practical one. The P vs NP asks, is checking and proving equally hard? So I asked myself, is having taste enough to sew a dress? Also asking myself how one approaches big, seemingly unmanageable problems, with a big lack in expertise. How do you even start working on it with no end in sight? Well let me tell you something. You don't.

You sit there and think: you will do it later, it is too hard, you will not succeed anyway, I should have chosen something easier, I should have given up.

And a more modern approach, you sit there and think, can ChatGPT do it instead? Sadly for me Al can't yet sew dresses.

What inspired me to do this in the first place, was an experience from my computer science studies. One of my courses focused on the time complexity classes of algorithms, which P and NP are by the way. It was said that anyone who solves the P vs NP problem during the exam, will get full marks without actually answering any exam questions. So we joked that we simply have to solve it at home and write it down during the exam. One peer pessimistically commented, that we will not manage to solve it, people much smarter than us have tried and failed. That sentiment is realistic, a very rational statement. But to be creative, is to transcend limits, to shoot for the moon and probably land on your butt instead.

As a continuation of the Harlem Renaissance and in defiance of the racism present in drag competitions, Ballroom culture emerged in the 1960s in Harlem, New York, founded by Black and Latinx trans women —most notably Crystal LaBeija. What began as beauty competitions gradually evolved through the addition of new categories, and by the late 1980s the performance category had become established. Performance is a form of "dance" in which participants express themselves through exaggerated poses, traditional movements, and bold dance elements.

Although the performance category has undergone many changes, the first established style of voguing was Old Way, characterized by clean lines, symmetry, precision, and grace. Later, more fluid and flexible movements were introduced into the style known as New Way. Finally, around 1995, Vogue Fem—the most widely recognized style of voguing today— emerged, known for its feminine movements, dramatic expression, and storytelling elements.

Through my nearly two years of participation in the Ballroom culture, I have frequently encountered discussions about the development of vogue from Old Way to Vogue Fem. Since Old Way is also my own style of performance, this project aims to visualize and bring the movements to life—in order to truly draw the stories told by Voguers and try to see how and if the two styles align. And perhaps gain a perspective into answering: How much of Old Way is still visible in Vogue Fem?

What happens to experiences once they become memory?

While memories are often triggered by visual cues or a familiar scent, this work explores sound as a trigger for recollection. By revisiting experiences through sound alone, the project explores how memories can shift over time and how repeated remembering can reshape what is recalled.

The project began by recording the sounds of each new experience. Rather than using photographs or videos, those recordings became the only way of returning to the experience. Immediately afterwards, sketches were created to visualize the moments that stood out most. Later, while listening to the recordings again, new sketches were made, this time trying actively to remember the moments in which the sounds had been recorded.

The final work consists of three pieces. The first depicts the memory of attending a concert alone, the second shows the experience of a multi-day train journey through Europe and the third combines the revisits of each experience into a single composition. Accompanied by a soundtrack built from the archived recordings, the pieces reveal how memories evolve through recollection. Rather than remaining fixed, memories can change each time they are revisited. The recurring symbols and changing meanings within the drawings show that remembering is not the retrieval of a perfect record but an ongoing process of reconstruction.

In this project, I explore how nature changes through human activity, using the mountain bike trails at Teufelsberg in Berlin as a case study. I find this location particularly interesting because, on the one hand, it is a naturally grown forest, while on the other, it is heavily shaped by recreational activities. Every use of the forest leaves traces, and mountain biking is no exception. Trails become wider, the soil becomes compacted, vegetation disappears, and new paths continue to emerge.

I visited Teufelsberg several times, exploring it both on foot and by mountain bike. During these visits, I collected video and audio recordings that capture the different conditions of the site. I also used drone footage to reveal the structure of the trail network and its expansion throughout the forest from an aerial perspective.

The video is a personal investigation of a place that I both use and observe. Rather than simply documenting these changes, I want to make them visible and tangible for the viewer. The work invites the audience to reflect on how everyday human activities shape natural landscapes, often in ways that go unnoticed.

"Weltschmerz" is a German expression that describes "a mood of weariness or sadness about life arising from the acute awareness of evil and suffering". Unlike with other emotions, the artist found himself unable to process the feeling of Weltschmerz. Neither introspection nor activism lead to more than temporary relief, leaving him permanently exposed to it.

This work explores the feeling of Weltschmerz by means of animation and sound. The constant flow of negative information hammering down on each one of us results in the suffering of all those who consume it. Through digital means, we ingest small doses of shock about the state of the world and hopelessness about the future at a time; for many of us while enjoying the incredible privilege of sitting safely in our homes.

We are caught in hollow absurdity, unable to escape the feeling or exposure to what causes it. Visitors can use the headphones next to the installation to experience a re-creation of this absurdity in sound and image. The almost dadaist soundscape accompanied by jarring visuals invokes the same despair as Weltschmerz does, though at a higher dosage. This work does not present a solution or a way to escape this negative emotion, it is merely descriptive; an attempt to better understand its effects.

The ocean is often perceived as vast, resilient, and seemingly untouched by human activity-an inexhaustible resource capable of absorbing the consequences of human consumption due to its immensity.

Through her artistic practice, Paula de la Nuez García challenges this perception, exploring the overlooked consequences of environmental degradation and the strong dependence between people and the natural world. Through painting, she invites viewers to consider both the beauty and fragility of the ecosystems that sustain life, while also serving as a wake-up call to the urgency of environmental action.

In //:shift-ing TIDES, the artist presents a triptych portraying the same underwater environment across a gradual process of change. Beginning with a scene characterized by abundance and biodiversity, the series moves towards a landscape increasingly marked by pollution before arriving at a final image of complete emptiness. Through this progression, the work contrasts presence and absence, inviting viewers to confront the consequences of environmental neglect.

This artwork explores a question that has accompanied me throughout this project: Can true charity exist, or are human actions always driven by self-interest?

At the beginning of my research, I believed altruism and egoism were opposites. As I continued researching, I realised that human motivation is rarely simple. People may help others out of empathy while also experiencing fulfilment and personal growth. Rather than diminishing the act, these feelings may naturally accompany compassion.

Inspired by Leonardo da Vinci's Vitruvian Man, my work reinterprets the ideal human figure as a symbol of contradiction and complexity rather than perfection. The interconnected elements surrounding the figure represent the emotional, ethical, and social influences that shape our decisions.

Instead of offering a clear answer, the artwork invites viewers to reflect on whether an act of kindness becomes less genuine if it also benefits the person giving. Perhaps the ability to care for others while simultaneously growing ourselves is one of the defining characteristics of being human.

Memoryscapes is a multi-sensory installation exploring nostalgia, memory, and the emotional distance between a person and the place they call home. The work focuses on people who cannot return to their childhood homes, hometowns, or places of origin as often as they wish because of migration, war, political instability or physical distance.

The installation is built from fragments of remembered places rather than a realistic reconstruction. Still and moving scenes are layered together to create an atmosphere of return. The still images suggest frozen memories or moments that remain fixed in the mind, while the videos bring movement, rhythm and everyday life back into the space. These collected materials do not try to recreate one fixed place, but instead form a shifting emotional landscape made from personal memories and sensory traces. The transition between scenes uses a pixelate effect. This choice reflects the way memories often appear incomplete, blurred, or broken into small pieces before they become recognizable again. The bloom effect is used to create softness, warmth, and emotional intensity, giving some scenes a dreamlike quality rather than a purely documentary feeling.

The project asks how sensory experiences can bring back emotional memories stored in the body. A voice, a street sound, or a familiar color can sometimes create a stronger connection than a clear image. In this way, nostalgia is treated not only as mental memory, but as something physical and emotional. The work also reflects on how memories can be incomplete, changed or softened over time, while still carrying a strong emotional presence. Memoryscapes invites visitors to enter a temporary space of recollection. They may not share the exact memories shown in the work, but they are invited to connect with the feeling of missing a place, searching for belonging, and briefly returning through fragments of sound, image and atmosphere.

For eight days, I documented my emotional state through a daily journal. Instead of relying on conventional emotion words, I translated my feelings into colours, shapes, textures, memories, places, and seemingly unrelated associations. As the days passed, unexpected patterns began to appear. Some felt intuitive, others almost random. Rather than forcing them to make sense, I chose to trust them as part of my own emotional language.

The journal gradually developed into a personal visual vocabulary, one that often felt more honest than language itself.

The final work translates these journal entries into a collage composed of magazine cut-outs. Alongside it, an Al-generated image responds to the exact same emotional notes. Rather than asking which image is "better," the work explores how emotions change when they are interpreted through different ways of making images. What happens when an intensely personal emotional language is translated by a machine? Can an algorithm engage with emotional abstraction, or does it simply reproduce familiar visual patterns? And what does it mean if I find myself relating more to the Al's interpretation than to my own?

This work is less about finding answers than about observing what happens when feelings leave the mind and become images. It invites the viewer to consider whether emotions can ever be translated completely, or whether something always remains unspoken.

Die DA! tries itself to offers a glimpse into a partly fictional near future, in which right-wing nationalists stabilize their power through constantly escalating aggression. The title plays on a common, dehumanizing phrase in German "die da" ("those over there") through which exclusion is prepared linguistically long before it is enacted politically.

At the core of the game lies a mechanism that repeats itself endlessly in reality: a problem is blamed on a group, that group is removed, the problem persists and the search for the next scapegoat begins again, each round more absurd than the last.

"2029, it is time! It is on us to save this country! We pay too much for health care. Once we get rid of them, everything will be fine."... "We already got rid of them? Then it must be those lazy part-time insects."... "The insects are gone too, and prices are still up? It's the left-handed. Always has been." "Left-handed people are banned now, yet somehow my butter still costs 6 Euros? Then it's the ones who don't sort their recycling correctly." ... "See, the costs are already going down, well almost, but most certainly definitively, as long as we keep fighting. One more group. Just one more..."

By the game's end, the "enemy" has cycled through so many groups that the accusation itself becomes the only constant proof that the target was never really the point.

I started this project with a plan: build a synth from scratch, learn the ins and outs of oscillators, and feel like a proper electronic wizard. Reality laughed. The circuit gave me noise, silence, and a lot of head-scratching. So I took a breath, stepped back, and asked myself: what do I actually want?

Turns out, I've always wanted to move sound. Not just press keys or turn knobs, but let my hands shape the music directly-like conducting air. That idea felt too big for years, so I never started. But with the clock ticking and a healthy dose of deadline-fueled momentum, I finally dove in.

I built a little motion-capture system in Python that turns the tilt and roll of my hand into MIDI messages. Now my gestures control filter sweeps, resonance, all the juicy stuff in Ableton. It's not the synth I set out to make, but it's the thing I secretly dreamed of. And honestly? It works better because it's mine-wonky, improvised, and alive.

This performance is me letting go of perfection and just playing. No right way, no wrong way-just my hands, some code, and a lot of love for sound. I'm a trans woman, and my voice and my movements are finally starting to feel like they belong together. This piece is a celebration of that messy, beautiful alignment.

Come watch me wave my arms, shake my hands and make noise. It's going to be fun.

Interpersonal relationships are difficult to define. They rarely consist of single, defining moments, but rather arise from everyday encounters, shared routines, and small gestures. Sharing a coffee, a cup of tea, a meal, a message like "Hey, I was thinking of you," or playing a game together creates moments of closeness in which exchange, trust, and connection flourish.

For this work, everyday packaging and paper were collected and processed by hand into new paper. Materials that originally accompanied fleeting moments of use become a new medium for memories and connections. The diverse colors aswell as the varying textures reflect the diversity of interpersonal relationships. Some appear calm and harmonious, while others are marked by ruptures, friction, or intensity.

During the drying process, various objects, such as a cell phone, keys, or a dice were placed beneath the paper, or the material was manually shaped. The result is not a direct imprint of the objects, but rather subtle raised areas, indentations, and folds: traces. The objects used symbolize various forms of connection: the cell phone enables closeness despite distance, the key represents shared spaces and trust, and the dice symbolizes time spent together and playful encounters. What matters here are not the objects themselves, but the relationships and memories associated with them.

The sheets of paper are sewn together. The act of sewing becomes a visible gesture of connection: the individual fragments remain recognizable as distinct parts, yet form a cohesive whole. Empty spaces were deliberately left. They represent what has not yet been written: future encounters, open possibilities, and relationships yet to develop. At the same time, they refer to those moments in which closeness arises not from words, but from the shared experience of silence.
